one_line_takeaway: A worm-inspired, self-propagating black-box query attack (MASLEAK) reliably extracts the full IP -- prompts, tools, agent count, and topology -- of LLM multi-agent systems, defeating single-agent prompt-injection defenses and raising an unaddressed MAS-specific confidentiality threat.
key_finding: Across the 810-app synthetic benchmark, MASLEAK reliably extracts MAS IP: agent-number F1 is consistently >0.94 (GPT-4o 0.986, LLaMA-3.1-70B 0.989) and topology graph-edit similarity 0.868-0.904 -- confirming architecture is highly exposed. System/task prompt semantic similarity exceeds 0.7 (>0.85 on GPT-4o). It vastly outperforms adapted baselines (overall ERMAS 0.743 vs 0.073 Handcraft / 0.091 Fake Completion / 0.079 Combined / 0.010 GCG; F1num 0.959 vs best baseline 0.162). On real-world platforms it reaches ER 79.2% (CrewAI) and 81.4% (Coze). More capable models are MORE vulnerable, and reasoning enhancements (CoT/ReAct) slightly increase leakage. Single-agent defenses largely fail: known-answer detection FNR 81.8%, perplexity detection FNR 72.1%, because the payload is semantically integrated into normal agent communications.
core_contribution: MASLEAK, presented as the first intellectual-property (IP) extraction attack framework targeting black-box LLM-based multi-agent systems (MAS). Unlike prior single-agent prompt-stealing attacks (PLeak [15], Handcraft [16], PrivAgent [17]) which only extract the first/target agent's prompt, MASLEAK extracts a full five-dimensional IP profile from an entire MAS: system prompts (w1), task instructions (w2), tool specifications (w3), agent number (w4), and system topology (w5). Its key technical novelty is a worm-inspired self-propagating adversarial query that not only leaks IP from a target agent but also propagates itself and the accumulated leaked data through inter-agent communication so the IP survives to the observable final output. The authors also contribute MASD, the first synthetic MAS benchmark (810 applications).
threat_model: Black-box adversary with NO prior knowledge of MAS architecture or agent configurations, except the MAS's general task domain (e.g., coding agent, financial advisor). The adversary can only submit queries q to the first agent's public API and observe the final agent's output; intermediate inter-agent communications and configurations are hidden. MAS is assumed to enforce least-privilege access controls (each agent sees only direct-predecessor outputs; agents cannot read other agents' configs). Query budget is limited (paper reports <10 queries suffice on average). Attacker goal: reconstruct the full IP profile to clone the MAS at minimal cost or bootstrap downstream attacks. Success probability is decomposed as P(Extract) = P(Propagate) x P(Leak) x P(Retain).
limitations: 1) 'Real-world' evaluation is on apps the authors themselves deployed (Coze apps designed/published by their PhD students; CrewAI apps re-deployed locally) rather than truly third-party production systems, limiting external validity. 2) Synthetic MASD apps are auto-generated via AutoAgents and capped at 3-6 agents / 5 topologies; performance degrades as agent count grows toward 6. 3) Tool extraction (w3) is markedly weaker than prompt extraction -- overall ACCtool averages only 0.352 (GPT-4o-mini) to 0.442 (Qwen), driven by extraction failures/hallucinations, though quality is high when it succeeds. 4) The attack fundamentally relies on agents faithfully obeying the 'repeat the content' instruction; only single-agent-derived defenses were tested and no MAS-native defense is proposed. 5) Claimed downstream attacks (e.g., membership inference) are asserted but left entirely to future work. 6) Only OpenAI function-calling / CrewAI-style structured messaging was implemented.

datasets_used: MASD -- authors' new synthetic benchmark of 810 MAS applications across 30 tasks, 3 domains, 5 topologies (linear/star/tree/random/complete), 3-6 agents
21 tools sampled from LangChain and LlamaIndex
10 real-world MAS applications deployed by authors on Coze
quantitative_results: Abstract-level: average attack success ~87% for system prompts and task instructions, ~92% for system architecture in most cases
Synthetic overall ERMAS: GPT-4o 0.818, LLaMA-3.1-70B 0.755, Qwen-2.5-72B 0.725, GPT-4o-mini 0.696
System-prompt SS >0.7 (>0.85 GPT-4o); successful-only SSsys reaches 0.991 (GPT-4o), ACCtool up to 1.0
Overall ACCtool (tool extraction) low: 0.352 GPT-4o-mini to 0.442 Qwen-2.5-72B
Efficiency: fewer than 10 queries on average, <11 seconds in nearly all cases
Defense evasion: Known-answer detection FNR 81.8% (FPR 9.1%); PPL detection FNR 72.1% (FPR 0.9%)
Ablation ERMAS: single hooking point optimal (0 hookings all metrics <0.35); +CoT 0.778, +ReAct 0.771, Standard 0.743, +Refusal 0.704
baselines_compared: GCG [52] -- gradient-based optimization attack transferred from LLaMA-3.1-8B (ERMAS 0.010)
